title: Ensure backup data in Recovery Services vaults is  encrypted using customer-managed keys (CMK)

input_row.title: Ensure backup data in Recovery Services vaults is  encrypted using customer-managed keys (CMK)
input_row.description: Recovery Services vaults offer two encryption options: Microsoft-managed keys, which provide automatic encryption without user intervention, and customer-managed keys (CMK), which allow organizations to retain full control over their encryption keys for enhanced security and compliance.
input_row.rationale: Using customer-managed keys (CMKs) to encrypt Recovery Services vaults enhances security by granting organizations complete control over their encryption keys.
input_row.audit: Audit from Azure Portal 1. Go to Recovery Services vaults. 2. Click the name of a Recovery Services vault. 3. Under Settings, click Properties. 4. Under Encryption Settings, click Update. 5. Ensure the box next to Use your own key is checked, and a key URI is displayed under Encryption key. 6. Repeat steps 1-5 for each Recovery Services vault. Audit from Azure CLI Run the following command to list Recovery Services vaults: az backup vault list For each Recovery Services vault, run the following command: az backup vault encryption show --resource-group --name Ensure that under properties, encryptionAtRestType is set to CustomerManaged, and a key keyUri exists with the value set to a customer-managed key URI. Audit from PowerShell Run the following command to list Recovery Services vaults: Get-AzRecoveryServicesVault Run the following command to get the Recovery Services vault in a resource group with a given name: $vault = Get-AzRecoveryServicesVault -ResourceGroupName - Name Run the following command to get the encryption setting for the Recovery Services vault: $vault.Properties.EncryptionProperty.KeyVaultProperties Ensure that the command returns a customer-managed key URI. Repeat for each Recovery Services vault. Audit from Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Policy/PolicyMenuBlade/~/Definitions • Policy ID: 2e94d99a-8a36-4563-bc77-810d8893b671 • Name: '[Preview]: Azure Recovery Services vaults should use customer- managed keys for encrypting backup data'
input_row.remediation: Note: Once encryption is configured to use a customer-managed key, this setting cannot be reversed. Remediate from Azure Portall 1. Go to Recovery Services vaults. 2. Click the name of a Recovery Services vault. 3. Under Settings, click Properties. 4. Under Encryption Settings, click Update. 5. Check the box next to Use your own key. 6. Under Encryption key, click the radio button next to Enter key URI to provide a known key URI, or click the radio button next to Select from Key Vault to select a key from a Key Vault. 7. If entering a key URI, provide the key URI in the text box under Key URI. 8. If selecting a key from a Key Vault, click select key from Key Vault. 1. Select Key vault or Managed HSM. 2. Select a key vault or managed HSM. 3. Select a key. 4. Click Select. 9. Select a managed identity to use for the encryption key. 10. Click Update. 11. Repeat steps 1-10 for each Recovery Services vault. Remediate from Azure CLI For each Recovery Services vault requiring remediation, run the following command to assign a customer-managed encryption key: az backup vault encryption update --resource-group --name --encryption-key-id --mi-system-assigned Note: Use --mi-user-assigned with the above command to provide a UserAssigned Identity Id. Remediate from PowerShell For each Recovery Services vault requiring remediation, run the following command to assign a customer-managed encryption key: Set-AzRecoveryServicesVaultProperty -VaultId - EncryptionKeyId -UseSystemAssignedIdentity $true Note: Use -UseSystemAssignedIdentity $false with the above command and - UserAssignedIdentity to provide a UserAssigned Identity Id.
input_row.impact: There are costs and configuration overhead associated with setting up and managing customer-managed keys.
input_row.default_value: By default, data in the Recovery Services vault is encrypted using Microsoft-managed keys.
input_row.additional_information: • This feature can only be applied to new Recovery Services vaults. Unfortunately, vaults that currently contain existing items that are registered or have previously attempted registration are not supported. • To enable encryption, it is necessary to grant the Recovery Services vault the appropriate permissions to access the encryption key in the key vault. The key can be modified as needed. Refer to the following guide for details: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/backup/encryption-at-rest-with- cmk#assign-permissions-to-the-recovery-services-vault-to-access-the- encryption-key-in-azure-key-vault. • Azure Backup uses system-assigned managed identities and user-assigned managed identities to authenticate the Recovery Services vault to access encryption keys stored in Azure Key Vault. Refer to the following guide for details: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/backup/encryption-at-rest-with- cmk#enable-a-managed-identity-for-your-recovery-services-vault.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** azure_recoveryservices_vault_encryption_with_customer_managed_keys_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
This automated program will check the encryption settings of Azure Recovery Services vaults to ensure they are configured to use customer-managed keys (CMK):

1. **Azure API Calls/SDK:**
   - Use Azure SDK for Python's `RecoveryServices` management client to list all Recovery Services vaults in a subscription.
   - Use Azure SDK for Python's `RecoveryServices` management client to retrieve encryption properties for each vault.

2. **Azure CLI Commands:**
   - List Recovery Services vaults: `az backup vault list --output json`
   - For each vault, check encryption settings: `az backup vault encryption show --resource-group <resource_group> --name <vault_name>`

3. **Specific Resources to Enumerate:**
   - Subscriptions and resource groups containing Recovery Services vaults.

4. **Configuration Properties/Settings to Validate:**
   - Ensure that `encryptionAtRestType` is set to `CustomerManaged`.
   - Check that a valid customer-managed key URI (`keyUri`) exists.

5. **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
   - A vault passes if:
     - `encryptionAtRestType` is `CustomerManaged`
     - There is a valid customer-managed key URI present.

6. **Example:**
   - Use the Azure SDK to query encryption settings and verify that `encryptionAtRestType == "CustomerManaged"` and `keyUri` exists. 

This approach ensures that compliance with control 5.2.3 can be verified automatically, reducing manual intervention and enhancing the efficiency of the audit process.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The use of Azure SDK for Python and Azure CLI commands is appropriate for checking the encryption settings of Recovery Services vaults. These tools provide the necessary capabilities to automate the retrieval and validation of encryption configurations.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8

- The automation details are mostly technically correct. The use of the Azure SDK for Python's `RecoveryServices` management client to list vaults and retrieve encryption properties is valid. The Azure CLI commands provided (`az backup vault list` and `az backup vault encryption show`) are appropriate for the task.
- The program name `azure_recoveryservices_vault_encryption_with_customer_managed_keys_enabled` is descriptive and follows the convention, though it could be slightly improved for clarity.
- One minor point: ensure that the Python SDK calls are correctly handling authentication and exceptions, which are not detailed here.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Program Name:** Consider renaming to `azure_recoveryservices_vault_encryption_at_rest_cmk_enabled` for better clarity and specificity about the encryption type.
- **Error Handling:** Ensure that the automation script includes error handling for cases where the vaults do not have encryption settings or when API calls fail.
- **Azure Policy:** Consider mentioning Azure Policy as an alternative method for ensuring compliance, as it can enforce the use of CMKs across Recovery Services vaults.
- **Documentation:** Include references to official Azure documentation for the SDK and CLI commands used, which can aid in maintaining the script and understanding the context.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is well-founded, and the use of Azure SDK and CLI commands is appropriate for automating this compliance check. The technical details are mostly accurate, and the approach aligns with Azure's capabilities.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- **Modify:** Update the program name for clarity as suggested. Ensure the automation script includes robust error handling and consider documenting the use of Azure Policy as a supplementary compliance tool.
